lede: An agent is an LLM with permission, memory and tools. It decides what to do next, it acts, and it watches what happens — not just a sentence generator.

body.p: Three properties promote a model from chatbot to agent: (1) tool use — it can call typed functions, edit files, run shells; (2) a goal or task that survives between turns; (3) a loop that keeps running until the task is done or the loop is cut.

An agent is not a magic intern. It does not have judgement of consequence beyond what is encoded in its prompt, its tools, and its observable evidence. A capable model with reckless permissions is more dangerous, not less.

Orchestrator (P-12) — coordinates and delegates. Subagent — narrow scope, fresh context. Workforce agent — a role with a budget and an SLA. Gate agent (P-32) — a structured judgement at a pipeline stage. Sweep agent (P-33) — a periodic auditor. Each shape has different trust, cost and review needs.

Capability is not authority. Whether an agent may do X depends on the task fit (P-19), the model's trust profile (P-20) and the execution channel (P-21). Sandbox (P-16) and budget caps (P-17) are not optional — they are the precondition.

Frame the agent like a senior — role, standards, permission to refuse (avoid Anti-Pattern A-01). Use plan mode (P-08), then fire-track-review (P-23). Read the artefact, not the chat (P-15). Capture evidence so reviewers can trust without watching.
